Romanshorn

TGThurgau

Create Account (Free) Or try a free trial story first

Story Ideas

Each story is rooted in real local history and landmarks

Waldrata's gift -- the church on the horn

A noblewoman named Waldrata and her young son travel to the lake peninsula in the year 779 to dedicate their church to the monks of St. Gallen. A child discovers why the village was named for a 'horn' in the lake and what it means to give away everything you own for a promise of prayer and protection that will endure for a thousand years.

Create This Story

On 2 February 779, the noblewoman Waldrata and her son Waldbert donated their property in 'Rumanishorn' to the Abbey of St. Gallen, including a church dedicated to Mary, Peter, and Gallus. This is the first documented mention of Romanshorn. The name likely derives from a personal name ('Romanus') combined with 'Horn' (a peninsula jutting into Lake Constance). The donation bound Romanshorn to the powerful Benedictine monastery of St. Gallen for over a thousand years. The Alte Kirche (Old Church) is one of the oldest documented churches in the Thurgau region and is today a Swiss heritage site of national significance.

The coal-eating paddle steamer

In 1869, a child stowaway hides aboard the very first train ferry crossing Lake Constance, designed by the famous engineer John Scott Russell. As the paddle steamer devours coal at an alarming rate, the child must help the crew figure out why the engine is so ravenously hungry -- and make it across to the German shore before the fuel runs out.

Create This Story

On 22 February 1869, the Wuerttemberg State Railway and the Swiss Northeastern Railway opened a train ferry between Friedrichshafen and Romanshorn, one of the earliest international lake ferry services in Europe. The first vessel was a paddle steamer designed by Scottish engineer John Scott Russell, famous for co-designing the SS Great Eastern with Brunel. It could carry entire railway wagons across the lake but was soon nicknamed a 'coal eater,' consuming 600-720 kg of coal per crossing. It was retired in 1883. The train ferry service continued until 1976, when it was replaced by car ferries.

The turntable at midnight

In the old Locorama locomotive depot, a child discovers that at midnight the turntable still spins on its own. The old steam engines begin to whisper stories of the journeys they once made across the Swiss rail network -- from the first train in 1855 to the great goods runs across the lake. Each locomotive remembers a different era, and the child must ride them all before dawn.

Create This Story

Locorama is a railway museum housed in the former SBB locomotive depot near Romanshorn station, the very place where the railway arrived on 16 May 1855. The depot building dates from 1898 and includes a historic turntable and signal bridge with 8 signals. It was once an important turnaround point for goods trains running between Switzerland and Germany across the lake. Key exhibits include the Ec 3/5 tender steam locomotive (built 1912), a Bavarian S 3/6 express steam locomotive, and the electric Ae 6/6 and Ae 4/7 locomotives. The arrival of the railway in 1855 transformed the sleepy farming village of 1,408 inhabitants into a booming transport hub of 4,577 by 1900.

Zeppelins over the Bodensee

From Romanshorn's harbour, a child watches a strange silver shape rise from the opposite shore on a summer morning in 1900. Rowing across the lake, they meet the eccentric Count Zeppelin and help launch LZ 1 from its floating hangar. For eighteen extraordinary minutes, they see Lake Constance from the sky -- and understand why some dreams are too big to stay on the ground.

Create This Story

Directly across the lake from Romanshorn lies Friedrichshafen, birthplace of the Zeppelin airship. Count Ferdinand von Zeppelin began construction of his first rigid airship (LZ 1) in June 1898 in a floating wooden hangar on the lake at Manzell near Friedrichshafen. On 2 July 1900, LZ 1 made its maiden flight from the lake surface, lasting about 18 minutes and covering roughly 6 km, reaching an altitude of 410 metres. Residents of Romanshorn had a front-row view of the great airships rising from the opposite shore. The Zeppelin Museum Friedrichshafen is easily reachable from Romanshorn via the ferry in 41 minutes.

The simultaneum -- sharing a church

In 1577, Catholic and Protestant children in Romanshorn must learn to share the same ancient church -- taking turns, respecting each other's sacred objects, and finding common ground despite their families' bitter differences. A story about cooperation, tolerance, and the small daily acts that kept the peace inside one building for over three hundred years.

Create This Story

In 1528-1529, the Reformation reached Romanshorn. Pastor Itelhans Bertz and the entire congregation converted to the Reformed faith. Beginning around 1563, both Catholics and Protestants shared the Alte Kirche under a simultaneum arrangement confirmed by the Swiss Diet. This shared use lasted over 300 years. In 1872, the two denominations created separate cemeteries as the first spatial separation. In 1911, a new Protestant church was consecrated, and in 1913 a new Catholic church was built, ending the centuries-long practice. The Alte Kirche, dating to the 779 donation, is today a Swiss heritage site of national significance.

The ferry that connects two worlds

A child rides the MF Euregia back and forth across Lake Constance, discovering that each 41-minute crossing tells a different story. One trip reveals the era of the train ferries and coal smoke, another uncovers tales of smugglers slipping between nations, and a third introduces the families who depend on this floating bridge every single day of the year.

Create This Story

Today, three double-ended ferries -- MF Friedrichshafen, MF Romanshorn, and MF Euregia -- operate the Friedrichshafen-Romanshorn route year-round, 365 days a year at hourly intervals. The MF Euregia, built in 1996 at the Bodan-Werft in Kressbronn, is 60 meters long and 13 meters wide. The crossing covers approximately 13.6 km, the widest part of Lake Constance, in about 41 minutes. In 2009, the Euregia alone transported over 586,000 passengers, 63,300 cars, 56,200 bicycles, and 8,200 trucks. The ferry saves travellers a long drive around the lake and is often called a 'floating bridge' between Switzerland and Germany.

Joachim's rope

In 1836, a seventeen-year-old boy named Joachim Fatzer starts a rope factory in Romanshorn with nothing but hemp and ambition. A child follows the story of Fatzer's ropes from hand-twisted lines for lake fishermen to the steel cables holding the world's greatest cable cars, discovering that one thread of determination, spun strong enough, can hold up a mountain.

Create This Story

In 1836, Joachim Fatzer started a hemp rope factory in Romanshorn at the age of 17. The company grew with the steamship era, supplying ropes for the Lake Constance fleet. Around 1900, Fatzer AG transitioned to steel wire rope production, and by 1985 produced 2,000 tonnes of steel wire annually. Today Fatzer supplies wire ropes for world-record ropeways worldwide and remains headquartered in Romanshorn. The company's history mirrors the town's own transformation from agricultural village to industrial hub.

The secret of the Schlossberg

A child exploring the castle hill discovers that the 1404 castle, the ancient church with its mysterious red-chalk frescoes, and the lakeside Seepark are all connected by a hidden path. At every turn the path reveals a different century -- from the Prince-Abbot's administrator ruling in the castle to the fishermen mending nets on the shore -- until the child reaches the summit and sees all of Romanshorn's history spread out below.

Create This Story

The castle hill (Schlossberg) has been a focal point of Romanshorn since the medieval period, with historical images dating back to 1760 showing its prominence above the lake. The 'New Castle' was built in 1404 and served from 1367 to 1798 as the seat of the Prince-Abbot's regional administrator. The castle was rebuilt in 1617/18 and completely modernized in 1931. In May 2017 it reopened as the Hotel Schloss Romanshorn. The Alte Kirche nearby preserves 14th-century fresco fragments in red chalk on the choir's east wall. Both the castle and church are listed in the Inventory of Swiss Heritage Sites, and the Seepark stretches along the lakefront on the Schlossberg hillside.
